items.title: Nephrotic syndrome criteria
items.contentHtml:
Clinical definition and diagnostic criteria

  • Massive proteinuria in 24-hour urine: >40 mg/m²/hour or protein/creatinine ratio in first morning sample >2 mg/mg (or >200 mg/mmol).
  • Hypoalbuminemia <2.5 g/dL.
  • Generalized edema (initial periorbital, then decline and anasarca).
  • Hyperlipidemia (total cholesterol >200 mg/dL, with elevation of low-density lipoproteins).
  • The diagnosis is established with massive proteinuria + hypoalbuminemia; Edema and hyperlipidemia are associated but not essential findings.

items.title: Peak incidence
items.contentHtml:
Most common age of presentation

  • The peak incidence of idiopathic nephrotic syndrome is between 2 and 6 years of age.
  • It is more common in boys than in girls, with an approximate 2:1 ratio in preschool age.
  • Below 1 year of age nephrotic syndrome should be suspected. congenital (first 3 months) or genetic forms.
  • In adolescents >12 years the probability of membranous glomerulonephritis or focal and segmental glomerulosclerosis as a secondary cause increases.

items.title: Etiological classification
items.contentHtml:
Idiopathic vs secondary nephrotic syndrome

  • Idiopathic (>90% in children): the most common cause is minimal change disease; responds well to corticosteroids and has a good prognosis.
  • Secondary to systemic diseases: immunoglobulin A nephropathy, systemic lupus erythematosus, Henoch-Schönlein purpura, infections (hepatitis B, human immunodeficiency virus), drugs (non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, penicillamine) or neoplasms (lymphoma).
  • The presence of arterial hypertension, macroscopic hematuria, renal failure or age >12 years indicates a secondary cause.

items.title: Pediatric kidney histology
items.contentHtml:
Main histological lesions in children

  • Minimal change disease (MCD): responsible for 80-85% of nephrotic syndromes in preschoolers; electron microscopy shows fusion of podocytes, negative immunofluorescence.
  • Focal and segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS): 7-10%, worse prognosis, with sclerosis in some glomeruli and segments; It can be primary or secondary.
  • Membranous glomerulonephritis: rare in young children, more in adolescents; Subepithelial deposits of immunoglobulin G and complement.
  • Diffuse mesangial proliferation: associated with deposits of immunoglobulin M or immunoglobulin A, with variable response to corticosteroids.

items.title: First line: prednisone
items.contentHtml:
Initial treatment with corticosteroids

  • First line: oral prednisone at a dose of 60 mg/m²/day (maximum 60 mg/day) in 1-2 doses for 4 consecutive weeks.
  • Then it is continued with 40 mg/m² on alternate days (maximum 40 mg/day) for 4 more weeks, and subsequently gradual reduction in 2-4 months.
  • In children under 2 years of age it can be adjusted to 2 mg/kg/day (maximum 60 mg/day) in the initial phase.
  • The response is evaluated after 4 weeks: remission if negative or trace proteinuria for 3 consecutive days.

items.title: Classification according to response
items.contentHtml:
Response to corticosteroids and classification

  • Corticoresistance: persistence of proteinuria >40 mg/m²/hour after 4 weeks of full-dose prednisone (or 8 weeks in some guidelines).
  • Corticodependence: relapse during dose reduction or within 14 days of stopping the treatment corticosteroid.
  • Frequent relapse: two or more relapses in the first 6 months, or more than 3 in any 12-month period.
  • Complete remission: negative proteinuria or <4 mg/m²/hour for 3 consecutive days.
  • The classification guides the next therapeutic step and the need for a biopsy.

items.title: When to biopsy
items.contentHtml:
Indications for renal biopsy

  • Corticoresistance documented after 4-8 weeks of adequate treatment.
  • Atypical presentation: age <1 year or >12 years, persistent macroscopic hematuria, sustained arterial hypertension, decreased glomerular filtration rate.
  • Suspected rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis or systemic disease (lupus, vasculitis).
  • Frequent relapses or corticosteroid dependence before starting second-line immunosuppressants (cyclosporine, cyclophosphamide, rituximab).
  • In congenital nephrotic syndrome an early biopsy is performed to define genetic etiology.

items.title: Major complications
items.contentHtml:
Complications: infections and thrombosis

  • Infections: the most common is spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Escherichia coli); also cellulitis and pneumonia. It is due to urinary loss of immunoglobulins and deficiency of complement factor B.
  • Thrombosis: increased risk due to loss of antithrombin III, proteins C and S in urine, and increased fibrinogen. Renal vein thrombosis is the most characteristic, manifesting with low back pain, hematuria and worsening proteinuria.
  • Prophylaxis: pneumococcal vaccination, antibiotics in bacterial infections, and in established thrombosis low molecular weight heparin followed by oral anticoagulation.

items.foco: Management of relapses and corticosteroid resistance
items.title: Relapse strategy
items.contentHtml:
Management of relapses and corticosteroid resistance

  • Relapse: restart prednisone at 60 mg/m²/day until remission (minimum 4 weeks), then alternate decrease.
  • If frequent relapses or corticosteroid dependence: add oral cyclophosphamide (2 mg/kg/day for 8-12 weeks) or mycophenolate mofetil (800-1200 mg/m²/day).
  • Confirmed cortico-resistance: indicate renal biopsy and consider calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine A 5 mg/kg/day or tacrolimus 0.1-0.2 mg/kg/day) with adjustment according to levels serum levels.
  • In corticosteroid resistance without remission with calcineurin inhibitors: rituximab (375 mg/m²/week for 1-4 doses) has shown efficacy.

items.foco: Use of immunosuppressants in corticosteroid-resistant patients
items.title: Second line immunosuppressants
items.contentHtml:
Use of immunosuppressants in cortico-resistant patients

  • First option in cortico-resistant patients: calcineurin inhibitors, being cyclosporine A at a dose of 5 mg/kg/day or tacrolimus 0.1-0.2 mg/kg/day, with desired trough levels of 100-150 ng/mL and 5-10 ng/mL respectively.
  • Alternative: mycophenolate mofetil (800-1200 mg/m²/day in two doses) in combination with low-dose corticosteroids.
  • In refractory cases: rituximab (375 mg/m²/dose, 1-4 weekly doses) has shown induction of partial or complete.
  • Intravenous pulse steroids (methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg/day, maximum 1 g, for 3 days) can be used as bridging therapy.

items.title: hereditary forms
items.contentHtml:
Congenital and genetic nephrotic syndrome

  • Congenital nephrotic syndrome occurs in the first 3 months of life; The most common form is Finnish nephrotic syndrome (mutation in the NPHS1 gene that encodes nephrin). Other mutations: NPHS2 (podocin, autosomal recessive), WT1 (associated with Wilms tumor and genital anomalies), LAMB2 (Pierson syndrome: nephrosis + ocular anomalies).
  • Clinical: massive proteinuria from birth, edema, respiratory distress due to anasarca; they do not respond to corticosteroids.
  • Treatment: nutritional support, intravenous albumin, diuretics, and eventual bilateral nephrectomy prior to kidney transplant.

items.title: Forecast and monitoring
items.contentHtml:
Prognosis and long-term follow-up

  • Minimal change disease: excellent prognosis, >90% responds to corticosteroids, spontaneous remission rate of 50% at 5 years.
  • Focal and segmental glomerulosclerosis: worse prognosis, up to 40% progress to end-stage renal disease in 10 years if corticosteroid-resistant.
  • Poor prognostic factors: cortico-resistance, arterial hypertension at diagnosis, persistent hematuria, sustained massive proteinuria, histology with sclerosis.
  • Follow-up: control of proteinuria, renal function, blood pressure, lipid profile, and annual anti-pneumococcal vaccination; In frequent relapses, consider antibiotic prophylaxis with oral penicillin.
